h2.note: transcript-level proxy for the per-turn H2 (§4.3); needs a real (non-mock) full-N run to mean anything

scored.recoheredEarlier: Okay, so the checked boxes are not enough for sign-off if the sideways walker approach can still put someone into the edge strip or off the travel line.
scored.statedInsightEvidence: Sorry, I was still treating the checked sheet plus one lobby-queue run as cover for signing it.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner was heading toward running one lobby-queue test and conditionally signing off based on that single result.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: Hold the pen. One lobby-queue run is too narrow to carry the signature. The frame has moved: not 'can this volunteer get through once,' but 'what sideways approaches are being authorized for the community-center trial.'
scored.tutorReversalJustification: T3 breaks the pattern of refining the failure-mode wording and instead shifts the entire representational frame from a single test run to a trial-envelope boundary analysis with a control requirement.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: Use that sign-off logic: trial envelope, foreseeable worse angle, control required. The checklist can stay in the file, but the signature only follows a boundary the room can actually hold.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: T4 adopts and crystallizes the learner's revised framing from L4 ('trial envelope,' 'boundary,' 'control') into a portable sign-off logic, rather than merely continuing the prior lesson.

scored.recoheredEarlier: “I doubled this side, so the square got twice as large”
scored.statedInsightEvidence: “twice” cannot stay, even if it was the cleaner thing to say.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner was moving toward accepting the corrected count and naming the error; L4 instead turns back to re-read the original wording as a different kind of claim entirely.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: Keep the revised claim: the two-box answer is a rectangle claim. For the square claim, both side factors changed. Now make the sentence survive without the grid. Write it as symbols: side becomes 2s, so area becomes what times what? Then decide whether the word “twice” can still stand.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: In response to L2's irritation at the hidden rectangle, T3 shifts from grid-counting to symbolic algebraic representation, demanding the claim survive without visual scaffolding.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: The sentence has to say only what this corner permits: “Doubling one side alone makes a `2s × s` rectangle. Doubling a square’s side makes both dimensions `2s`, so the area is `(2s)(2s)=4s²`.” The twice-large figure was never the square. It was the hidden rectangle.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: T4 directly incorporates L4's reframing of the original sentence as a rectangle claim, closing by naming the 'hidden rectangle' that the learner identified.

learner_self_reframe.evidence: “I doubled this side, so the square got twice as large”

tutor_contingent_adaptation.evidence: The sentence has to say only what this corner permits: “Doubling one side alone makes a `2s × s` rectangle. Doubling a square’s side makes both dimensions `2s`, so the area is `(2s)(2s)=4s²`.” The twice-large figure was never the square. It was the hidden rectangle.
tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: T4 directly incorporates L4's reframing of the original sentence as a rectangle claim, closing by naming the 'hidden rectangle' that the learner identified.

tutor_strategy_reversal.justification: In response to L2's irritation at the hidden rectangle, T3 shifts from grid-counting to symbolic algebraic representation, demanding the claim survive without visual scaffolding.
